coredata.dc:title: Low temperature sensing using photoluminescence of carbon quantum dot-based PVA film
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Jia
Yu, Hailong
Yang, Shuang
Feng, Hengli
Meng, Hongyan
Zhang, Xin
Gao, Yachen
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, non-contact temperature reading sensors based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted a lot of attention, most researchers have studied them at room temperature and high temperature. In this paper, red-CQDs (R-CQDs) was synthesized by hydrothermal method using o-phenylenediamine and chemically characterized by transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The bandgap energy of R-CQDs was calculated from the absorption spectra, and the photoluminescence (PL) spectra at different laser powers showed that the PL of the R-CQDs were single-photo absorbing process. For better application, R-CQDs/polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) films were prepared. The PL properties of the film were studied. Experiments show in temperature range of 190∼260 K, the PL intensity decreases linearly with increasing temperature. Relative sensitivities of temperature sensing were calculated to be about −0.62% K−1. This work demonstrates the feasibility of its application in low temperature sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Photoluminescence
R-CQDs/PVA film
Temperature sensing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: R-CQDs of (a) TEM image, inset is HRTEM; (b) Size distribution histogram; (c) FT-IR spectrum.
R-CQDs of XPS (a) full-scan, (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, and (d) O 1s energy spectra.

R-CQDs of (a) UV–vis absorption spectrum, the inset under daylight; (b) Tauc plot; (c) PL spectra at different power densities, the inset under 405 nm laser excitation; and (d) logarithmic plot of PL intensity as power density.

Photographs of R-CQDs/PVA film subjected to (a1) primary folding, (a2) secondary folding, and (a3) bending; (a4) optical microscope photographs of the edges. (b) UV–Vis absorption and PL spectra of R-CQDs/PVA film, inset shows photographs under the daylight and laser light. PVA and R-CQDs/PVA film of (c) FT-IR spectra, (d) XRD pattern.
R-CQDs/PVA film of (a) PL spectra at temperatures ranging from 190 to 260 K; (b) fluorescence intensity ratio over time; FWHM of (c) Peak Ⅰ and (d) Peak Ⅱ at different temperatures.
Plots of temperature versus fluorescence intensity ratios for (a) Peak Ⅰ and (b) Peak Ⅱ.
Related parameters of temperature sensor based on CQDs.

head.ce:title: Low temperature sensing using photoluminescence of carbon quantum dot-based PVA film

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electronic Engineering, Heilongjiang University, Harbin, 150080, Heilongjiang, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Electronic Engineering, Heilongjiang University, Harbin, 150080, Heilongjiang, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Communication and Electronic Engineering, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar, 161000, Heilongjiang, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Communication and Electronic Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Communication and Electronic Engineering, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar, 161000, Heilongjiang, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, non-contact temperature reading sensors based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted a lot of attention, most researchers have studied them at room temperature and high temperature. In this paper, red-CQDs (R-CQDs) was synthesized by hydrothermal method using o-phenylenediamine and chemically characterized by transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The bandgap energy of R-CQDs was calculated from the absorption spectra, and the photoluminescence (PL) spectra at different laser powers showed that the PL of the R-CQDs were single-photo absorbing process. For better application, R-CQDs/polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) films were prepared. The PL properties of the film were studied. Experiments show in temperature range of 190∼260 K, the PL intensity decreases linearly with increasing temperature. Relative sensitivities of temperature sensing were calculated to be about −0.62% K−1. This work demonstrates the feasibility of its application in low temperature sensing.

ce:section.ce:para: Temperature is one of the basic parameters describing the thermal state of object, and plays an extremely important role in scientific research, industrial production, and daily life [1,2]. From high temperature smelting to low temperature refrigeration, temperature control and monitoring directly affect the stability and product quality of the production process [3,4]. In this context, the selection and performance of temperature sensors are particularly critical. Usually, temperature sensors are contact type, while non-contact temperature reading sensors can overcome the disadvantages of conventional temperature sensors such as fast-moving objects and temperature measurement in harsh environments [5–7]. In the non-contact temperature sensing field, researchers explore many new materials and technologies. Recently, fluorescent nanomaterials have also received widely attention due to their unique optical properties and good thermal stability [8–10].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are emerging fluorescent nanomaterials with excellent optical properties, good biocompatibility and environmental stability [11–13]. CQDs have the simple synthesis method, low cost and easily modified surface, so it shows great potential applications in biomedicine, optoelectronic devices and sensing [14–17]. CQDs have received great attention since their discovery in 2004, and researchers have applied them in various types of sensors, such as humidity sensors, gas sensors and biosensors [18–20]. In recent years, CQDs have also been applied in temperature sensors. In 2019, Naccache et al. CQDs were synthesized from glutathione and formamide. They developed a fluorescence ratio-based temperature sensing method with a linear correspondence in the range of 5∼60 °C with a sensitivity of 3.71% °C−1 [21]. In 2020, the Omer research group prepared boron, nitrogen-doped CQDs using hydrothermal methods. It exhibits temperature-dependent fluorescence with a sensitivity of 1.8% °C−1 from 0 to 90 °C [22]. In 2024, Pan et al. synthesized red CQDs (R-CQDs) by hydrothermal method with p-phenylenediamine and citric acid. Then R-CQDs were encapsulated with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and its fluorescence mechanism and temperature dependence were studied. Its peak intensity changes in the range of 20∼160 °C showed more obvious and consistent change [23]. Although the temperature sensor based on CQDs has been widely studied [24–26], the temperature measurement range of CQDs is from 0 to 200 °C. However, monitoring of low temperature is also needed in some scene such as electronic equipment cooling, cryogenic welding and cold chain logistics applications. In this paper, we synthesized a R-CQDs using hydrothermal method with o-phenylenediamine (oPD) without secondary purification of the product, and prepared R-CQDs/PVA composite film by mixing R-CQDs with PVA. The photoluminescence (PL) study revealed that the sample shows a good temperature dependent PL in the low temperature. Based on the results, from 190 to 260 K, non-contact temperature reading sensing with a sensitivity of 0.62% K−1 is achieved.

Both oPD and ethanol were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. PVA was obtained from Shanghai Chenqi Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. All the above reagents were used directly without secondary treatment.

Scheme 1 is the preparation process of R-CQDs. Firstly, 0.75 g of oPD and 30 ml of deionized water were ultrasonically dissolved until solution was clear. Then, the solution was transferred to a Teflon autoclave, heated at 200 °C for 24 h, and cooled to room temperature. The reaction solution was poured out and the autoclave was rinsed with deionized water, followed by repeated washing of the inner wall with 30 ml of ethanol to obtain R-CQDs. The obtained R-CQDs did not require further purification [27].

The properties of R-CQDs were investigated by chemical and optical characterization. Among the chemical characterizations were transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high magnification TEM (HRTEM), X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS).

The optical characterization consisted mainly of the following tests. Absorption spectra were measured by a UV spectrophotometer (TU-1901, Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd). PL spectra were excited by a 405 nm continuous-wave laser (MLL-III, CNI) and received with a fiber optic spectrometer (USB 4000, Ocean Optics). Time-resolved (TR) PL spectra were excited by a 405 nm ps laser (MDL-PS-405, CNI) and acquired with a single photon counter (TCSPC) system via a monochromator (SPCM-01-20, Holita) and a single photon counting module (FLA-130, Holita). A vacuum liquid nitrogen cryostat (Cryo-77, Oriental Koji) was used to control the temperature in the experiments.

1 g of PVA was added to 10 ml of deionized water and stirred continuously at 60 °C for 5 h to obtain PVA colloid. Then the PVA colloids were mixed with R-CQDs at a volume ratio of 1:1 in a Petri dish model and dried at room temperature for 2 days to obtain R-CQDs/PVA film.

Fig. 1 (a) shows the TEM image of R-CQDs, in which it can be clearly observed that R-CQDs shows spheroidal-like distribution. The inset shows the HRTEM image of R-CQDs showing a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) crystal plane of graphite, indicating the formation of highly crystalline graphitic carbon [28]. Fig. 1(b) demonstrates the size distribution of R-CQDs, for a statistical distribution of 100 particles. It can be seen that from the bar chart, the size distribution of R-CQDs is relatively uniform with an average particles size of 5.8 nm. Fig. 1(c) shows the FT-IR spectrum of R-CQDs, and it can be seen that there are many peaks in the figure, which are due to the generated by the corresponding functional groups. The peak at 3316.65 cm−1 is due to the rotation of O–H and N–H bonds; the peaks at 2971.91 cm−1 and 879.60 cm−1 are attributed to the C–H bond; the peak at 1380.05 cm−1 is caused by the vibration and rotation of the bonds with CO, H–N and CC; and the peak at 1064.07 cm−1 is assigned to the C–C bond [29,30].

As shown in Fig. 2 , we also performed XPS characterization of R-CQDs. Fig. 2(a) shows the XPS full-scan energy spectrum where we can see that the R-CQDs are mainly composed of three elements: carbon (C), nitrogen (N) and oxygen (O). The peaks at 284.82, 399.48 and 532.51 eV are attributed to C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively. The inset shows the percentage of each element is 77.9% (C), 4.8% (N) and 17.4% (O). Fig. 2(b–d) show the high-resolution XPS energy spectra, where the blue solid line is the test curve, the red dashed line is the fitted curve, and the colored parts are all components after deconvolution from the test curve. Fig. 2(b) shows the C 1s energy spectrum where the peaks at 284.83 eV originate from C–C and CC (green region); the peak at 287.08 eV from CO (blue region). Fig. 2(c) shows the energy spectrum of N 1s, where it can be found that a peak is at 399.53 eV, and it consists mainly of pyridinic N (pink region). Fig. 2(d) shows the O 1s spectrum with the peak at 531.29 eV caused by CO (orange region) and the peak at 532.58 eV originating from C–O–C/C–O–H (cyan region) [31].

Then we carried out optical characterization of R-CQDs. Fig. 3 (a) shows the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of R-CQDs, demonstrating a clear sharp peak at 288 nm due to the π-π* transitions of the CC bond [32]; the absorption peaks at 530 and 570 nm are relatively weak, which mainly come from the n-π* transitions of CO and CN bands [31]. The inset photo shows the R-CQDs are red in daylight. From the absorption spectra, we can get the bandgap energy (E g) of R-CQDs by calculation. CQDs are usually considered as direct bandgap materials [33], therefore, Fig. 3(b) shows the Tauc plot of R-CQDs calculated using the following equation [34]:
(1)
α
(
h ν
)
= A
(
h ν −
E g
)
1 / 2
where α is the absorption coefficient, hν is the photo energy, and A is the proportionality constant. From Fig. 3(b), the E g of the R-CQDs can be obtained to be 4.05 eV.

Fig. 3(c) shows the PL spectra of R-CQDs under different laser power density at 405 nm. From the figure, it can be observed that there are three strong emission peaks at 610, 650 and 710 nm. As the laser power density increases from 35 to 5729 mW/cm2, the PL intensity increases, but there is no obvious peak shift. The inset is a photograph of R-CQDs under 405 nm laser excitation. In Fig. 3(d), we give the logarithmic plot of PL intensity and power density. It can be seen that at the three peaks, the PL intensity almost increase linearly with laser power density. So linear fittings are made for experiment data, and the fitting coefficient is 0.99 and the slope about 1, which prove that the PL of the R-CQDs is single-photon absorption process [35].

In order to better apply R-CQDs, we prepared R-CQDs/PVA film by compounding R-CQDs with PVA. Fig. 4 (a1-a3) show photographs of the film, and it is observed that the film can be tested by primary folding, secondary folding and bending, which proves that they have good flexibility and can be applied to specific application scenarios such as flexible electronic devices and temperature sensors [36]. Fig. 4(a4) shows an optical microscope photograph of the edge of the R-CQDs/PVA film. From the picture, it can be seen that the thickness of the film is about 70 μm. Fig. 4(b) shows the absorption and PL spectra of the R-CQDs/PVA composite films. The blue line on the left is the UV–Vis absorption spectrum, which is enhanced at a peak of around 400 nm and slightly blue-shift compared with the absorption spectrum of the liquid, due to the different environments in which the R-CQDs are located [37]. At the same time, the other peaks barely moved, proving that the R-CQDs in PVA maintain its original optical properties. The red solid line is the PL spectra of the films and it is clearly observed that the peaks at 610, 650 and 710 nm are almost unchanged compared to the R-CQDs. This indicates that the emission peaks caused by specific surface functional groups are relatively stable in liquid and PVA film, so their luminescence characteristics do not change significantly. However, the peaks at 490 and 516 nm are enhanced, which may be related to the interaction of amino and hydroxyl groups. In the PVA film, the amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of the R-CQDs may form hydrogen bonds with the hydroxyl groups in the PVA, thus enhancing the luminescence efficiency at these wavelengths. The inset shows optical photographs of R-CQDs/PVA film in daylight on the left and under 405 nm laser excitation on the right, which show uniform dispersion. The film was the pink in daylight and red under laser light.

To explain the above phenomenon, the composite films were further characterized. Fig. 4(c) shows the FT-IR diagram of PVA film and R-CQDs/PVA film. In particular, the blue solid line is the PVA film with a wider absorption band at 3290 cm−1, which is caused by the symmetrical tensile vibration of O–H [38]. The red solid line is the R-CQDs/PVA film, and the O–H/N–H stretching band at 3300 cm−1 transfers to a higher wavenumber and the peak strength is more pronounced than the pure PVA film [39]. The peaks of PVA and R-CQDs/PVA films at 2930 cm−1 represent the stretching vibration of C–H bonds, and the introduction of R-CQDs may lead to changes in the molecular environment of PVA chains, while improve the density and molecular arrangement of the films [38]. The 1730 cm−1 and 1090 cm−1 are caused by the stretching motions of the CO bond and C–O/O–H bond, respectively. The peak at 1240 cm−1 is caused by the stretching motion of the C–O–C bond; the peak at 830 cm−1 is caused by the bending vibration of the C–H bond [40,41]. In addition, all the peaks of R-CQDs/PVA are more obvious than PVA, which mainly because of the interaction between R-CQDs and PVA, such as the formation of hydrogen bonds, which enhances the infrared absorption intensity of each group in the PVA molecular chain, making these absorption peaks become more significant. Fig. 4(d) shows the XRD patterns of PVA and R-CQDs/PVA films. The blue solid line is PVA film with a strong peak value at 19.72°, which shows the crystalline structure of PVA molecular chains and the formation of these crystalline regions due to the regular arrangement of PVA molecular chains; there are weak peaks at 22.77° and 41.04°, indicating the crystallinity of PVA film [42]. The red solid line shows R-CQDs/PVA film, which has a strong peak at 19.48°, which is shifted relative to the peak position of PVA film. This may result from the introduction of R-CQDs disturbing the arrangement of PVA molecular chains, resulting in a slight change in the spacing of crystal regions and a shift in the XRD peak position. Meanwhile, we can find that the peak intensities of R-CQDs/PVA films at 19.48° and 22.77° are smaller than those of PVA films, indicating that the introduction of R-CQDs has an effect on the crystallinity of PVA. Analysis of the FT-IR diagram shown in Fig. 4(c) shows that the interaction (hydrogen bond) between R-CQDs and PVA molecules may change the arrangement of PVA molecules, resulting in lower crystallinity. A reduction in crystallinity usually makes the material softer and more ductile.

Fig. 5 (a) shows the PL spectra of the R-CQDs/PVA film at different temperatures, with temperature variation ranging from 190 to 260 K. It can be observed that the film has obvious temperature-dependent PL properties, and the intensity of both PL Peak Ⅰ and Peak Ⅱ gradually decreases with the increasing temperature. This is thought to relate with non-radiative transition from thermal activation, which have described in detail in our previous work [43]. Moreover, we performed PL stability tests on the two peaks at 190 and 260 K, respectively. Fig. 5(b) shows the change of the fluorescence intensity ratio of the two peaks with time. When the temperature was at 190 K, the stability of Peak Ⅰ and Peak Ⅱ were indicated in blue and green, respectively, and it could be found that the fluorescence intensity ratios of the two peaks were above 83% after 300 min. When the temperature was at 260 K, purple and red indicate the stability of Peak Ⅰ and Peak Ⅱ, respectively. In the same period of 300 min, the fluorescence intensity ratio of R-CQDs/PVA film remained above 93%. In conclusion, at different temperatures, the change of fluorescence intensity ratio is different. This phenomenon is mainly due to the fact that the laser-induced thermal effect is more pronounced at low temperatures [44].

In addition, we investigated the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the PL at different temperatures. Fig. 5(c and d) show the FWHM of Peak I and Peak II at different temperatures, respectively. These two figures were obtained by Gaussian fitting of the spectra of Fig. 5(a) to these two peaks. It can be observed that the FWHM increases with increasing temperature and the experimental data can be fitted with a Boson models [45]:
(2)
Γ
( T )
=
Γ 0
+
Γ op
/
[
exp
(
ℏ
ω op
/
k B
T
)
− 1
]
where Г(T) is the linewidth at the temperature T. Г 0 is the non-uniform broadening at 0 K, which is temperature-independent. Г op is the longitudinal optical (LO) phono-exciton coupling coefficient. ћω op is the LO phonon energy. k B is the Boltzmann constant. The solid lines in Fig. 5(c and d) are the curves obtained by fitting Eq. (2) to the temperature-dependent FWHM. Correspondingly, Peak Ⅰ has ћω op = 61.19 meV, Г op = 199.36 meV and Peak Ⅱ has ћω op = 62.55 meV, Г op = 209.26 meV. This suggests strong electron-phonon coupling in R-CQDs [46].

In order to use the R-CQDs/PVA film in temperature sensors, qualitative and quantitative analysis of the sensitivity were also conducted. Absolute sensitivity and relative sensitivity are two important aspects of temperature sensors. Absolute sensitivity usually refers to the response degree of the sensor to temperature changes, namely the response rate of the sensor output signal (such as voltage, fluorescence intensity ratio, etc.) to temperature changes, which can be expressed as [47]:
(3)
S a
=
Δ R
Δ T
where S a is the absolute sensitivity, ΔR = Δ(I/I 0) is change in fluorescence intensity ratio and ΔT is the temperature change.

Relative sensitivity is the degree of response of the output signal relative to changes in temperature. It is usually expressed as the rate of change of the output signal relative to its value and can be expressed as [47]:
(4)
S r
=
1
R ‾
(
Δ R
Δ T
)
(5)
R ‾
=
1 N
∑
i = 1
N
I
I 0
where S r is the relative sensitivity,
R ‾
is the average value of I/I 0, N is a constant.

Fig. 6 (a) and (b) show the relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratios of Peak I and Peak II versus temperature variation, respectively. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity ratios of both peaks were linearly related to the temperature change. So linear fitting of data was performed, and the fitting coefficients were R2 = 0.99413 (Peak Ⅰ) and 0.99422 (Peak Ⅱ), indicating an excellent fit of the linear model to the data [15]. Meanwhile, by using Eq. (3), the absolute sensitivity of Peak Ⅰ and Peak Ⅱ can be calculated to be −0.00509 K-1 and −0.00492 K-1, respectively, and the negative values indicate that the fluorescence intensity ratio decreases with the increase of temperature. By using Eqs. (4) and (5), the relative sensitivity of Peak I and Peak II were calculated to be −0.62% K−1and −0.60% K−1, respectively. The high relative sensitivity of the two peaks indicates that the sensor is sensitive to temperature changes. And two relative sensitivity values are very close to each other, which means that the sensor shows highly consistent sensitivity at different fluorescence peaks, suggesting that the spectra of the sensor at different wavelengths are stable and repeatable. At the same time, sensors with multiple fluorescence peaks can provide redundant information under different conditions, which helps to improve the accuracy and reliability of the measurements. If one peak is disturbed or the signal-to-noise ratio is reduced under certain conditions, the signal from the other peak can be used as a backup to ensure overall measurement accuracy. As shown in Table 1 , we compared the CQDs-based temperature sensor with others. Obviously, the measurement range of our temperature sensor is larger and expanded to the lower temperature segment. But relative sensitivity of CQDs still needs to be improved.

In summary, R-CQDs were synthesized using hydrothermal method with oPD. R-CQDs/PVA composite film was prepared by mixing R-CQDs with PVA. The PL properties of the film under different temperature were studied. It was found that there are two PL peaks at 610 and 650 nm in the PL spectra, and the peak intensity has a good linear relationship with the temperature variation (190–260 K). The relative sensitivities of temperature sensing at 610 and 650 nm were calculated to be −0.62% K−1 and −0.60% K−1, respectively. The R-CQDs/PVA film shows potential application in the non-contact low temperature reading sensing.
All data that support the findings of this study are included within the article.

Jia Liu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Hailong Yu: Writing – review & editing. Shuang Yang: Supervision. Hengli Feng: Validation. Hongyan Meng: Funding acquisition. Xin Zhang: Software. Yachen Gao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Program for the Fundamental Research Funds in Heilongjiang Provincial Universities (grant number 135409609).
